children.name: Corythaeola
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the largest of all turacos, a striking blue-grey bird with a tall, flattened black-and-blue crest. It is a highly social, frugivorous species of Central African forests.

children.name: Corythaeola cristata
children.description: The largest turaco, measuring up to 76 centimetres in length with a majestic blue-grey plumage, a tall black-tipped crest and a bright yellow bill and red-tipped tail. It inhabits the rainforest canopy of equatorial Africa from Guinea to Kenya. It moves in noisy flocks through the treetops, flapping lazily with deep, slow wingbeats.

children.name: Tauraco
children.description: Vividly coloured turacos with iridescent green upperparts, crimson flight feathers and a distinctive white or pale patch behind the eye. They are among the most brilliantly pigmented birds, owing their green colour to the unique copper-based pigment turacoverdin.

children.name: Tauraco corythaix
children.description: Endemic to the coastal and montane forests of South Africa, Swaziland and Mozambique, this brilliant green turaco sports a white-tipped crest and a crimson patch on the wing. It is a noisy but elusive inhabitant of the canopy, often betraying its presence with a loud, raucous 'kraak-kraak-kraak' as it bounds through the branches.

children.name: Tauraco livingstonii
children.description: Found in the lowland and montane forests of eastern Africa from Tanzania to South Africa, this species closely resembles the Knysna Turaco but has a taller, more pointed crest tipped with white. Its flight is direct, with rapid wingbeats interspersed with glides that show off the brilliant crimson flight feathers.

children.name: Tauraco leucotis
children.description: Distinguished by the large white patch on the side of the face and its green crest tipped with white, this turaco inhabits the highlands of Ethiopia and Eritrea. It prefers juniper and podocarp forests at elevations above 1,500 metres. Its deep, guttural croaking calls echo through the misty montane woodlands.

children.name: Musophaga
children.description: Deep violet-blue turacos with a vivid yellow or orange forehead and a heavy, domed bill. They are adapted to open woodlands and savannas rather than dense rainforest.

children.name: Musophaga violacea
children.description: A stunning deep violet-blue turaco with a bright yellow forehead and a heavy red-and-yellow bill, found across West Africa from Senegal to Nigeria. It prefers riverine forests and moist savanna woodlands. Despite its brilliant coloration, it is surprisingly well camouflaged among the dappled shadows of the canopy.

children.name: Musophaga rossae
children.description: Ranging from Cameroon and Angola to Kenya and Botswana, this deep violet turaco replaces the Violet Turaco in central and eastern Africa. It has a striking yellow bill and a prominent red-orange patch around the eye. It is an active, gregarious bird that travels in small flocks, clambering through the branches in search of fruits.

children.name: Gallirex
children.description: A small genus of turacos with a distinctive purple crest and a glossy green body, occupying the woodlands of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Gallirex porphyreolophus
children.description: A striking turaco with a glossy purple crest, iridescent green body and crimson wings, found from Uganda and Kenya to South Africa. It is a common inhabitant of riverine and coastal forests as well as well-wooded gardens. Its loud, resonant 'go-away' call is a familiar sound in the bushveld.
